definition: The difference between the highest and lowest frequency a medium can carry.
examTip: Do not confuse bandwidth with throughput.

definition: The number of signal cycles per second, measured in hertz.
examTip: Frequency is central to FDM, wireless channels, and RF behavior.

definition: The physical distance between matching points on consecutive signal cycles.
examTip: In fiber, WDM uses different light wavelengths as separate channels.

definition: Frequency-division multiplexing divides available bandwidth into separate frequency ranges.
examTip: Look for radio, TV, cable, or frequency-band clues.

definition: Time-division multiplexing gives each channel a fixed repeating time slot.
examTip: Fixed slots are the giveaway.

definition: Statistical time-division multiplexing assigns time slots dynamically to active channels.
examTip: Choose STDM when idle channels should not waste reserved slots.

definition: Wavelength-division multiplexing carries multiple light wavelengths across one fiber.
examTip: Wavelengths of light point to WDM.

definition: Coarse WDM uses wider wavelength spacing and lower-cost components.
examTip: Often associated with metro or cost-sensitive links.

definition: Dense WDM packs many tightly spaced wavelengths onto fiber for very high capacity.
examTip: Long-haul and very high capacity usually point to DWDM.

definition: Orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing splits data across many closely spaced subcarriers.
examTip: Common in Wi-Fi, LTE, and 5G contexts.

definition: Code-division multiple access separates users with unique spreading codes over shared spectrum.
examTip: Unique codes and spread spectrum point to CDMA.

definition: Carrier sense multiple access with collision avoidance, used by Wi-Fi to reduce wireless collisions.
examTip: Wireless avoids collisions; Ethernet detects them.

definition: A mobile device connects to a new cell before releasing the old one.
examTip: Make before break.

definition: A strong nearby transmitter can overpower a weaker distant transmitter sharing the same channel.
examTip: Power control is the usual mitigation.

definition: Optical time-domain reflectometer used to locate fiber faults and measure loss or reflections.
examTip: Choose OTDR for finding fiber break distance.

definition: Signal strength loss over distance or through a medium.
examTip: Repeaters or amplifiers may compensate for attenuation.

definition: Signal spreading over distance, which can blur pulses and limit fiber performance.
examTip: Dispersion affects timing and pulse clarity.

definition: Cyclic redundancy check, a strong error-detection method using polynomial division.
examTip: CRC is stronger than simple parity for burst errors.

definition: Automatic Private IP Addressing using 169.254.0.0/16 when DHCP is unavailable.
examTip: A 169.254 address usually means DHCP failed.

definition: Network Address Translation maps private internal addresses to one or more public addresses.
examTip: NAT lets private networks access the internet through public addresses.

definition: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol automatically assigns IP configuration.
examTip: DHCP reduces manual addressing errors.

definition: Domain Name System resolves hostnames to IP addresses.
examTip: If names fail but IPs work, suspect DNS.

definition: A documented record of normal performance and configuration.
examTip: Baselines make abnormal behavior visible.

definition: Acceptable use policy defining permitted and prohibited use of network resources.
examTip: AUP questions focus on user behavior expectations.

definition: A device or service that connects dissimilar networks or protocols.
examTip: A default gateway sends traffic off the local network.
